A Wedding Journey · Bir, Himachal Pradesh, India

Yaroslav&Tseyang

20 · June · 2026
Sukoon Baag Eco Farmstay · Bir Colony · Beneath the Dhauladhar Range

Tibet met Ukraine here. Now they'd like to meet you.

A Word from Us

Welcome to Bir

Bir is a small village tucked into the folds of the lower Himalayas — pine-covered ridges above, a quiet valley below, and the air carrying a hint of wood smoke and something ancient. For most of you, this will be your first time here. For Tseyang, it is home.

She arrived here as a child, one of thousands of Tibetans who made the journey across the mountains into India. The Tibetan colony — its monasteries, its people, the smell of butter lamps — is where she found ground again. That is why we are getting married here.

This guide is your soft landing. It covers what to expect, what to pack, how to get here, and what's worth doing while you are. For real-time help, there's a WhatsApp group — but start with this.

Ceremony Date
Saturday, 20 June 2026
Main Venue
Sukoon Baag Eco Farmstay
Laher, Bir Colony, Gunehr
Nearest Airport

~2.5 hrs to Bir

Sangrai Parking, Bir Colony
We will meet you there
Cameras
Film cameras provided for everyone — just bring your phone. We've got the rest.
Languages
English works everywhere. A smile works everywhere else.
Be Prepared

Weather & What to Pack

June in Bir is early Himalayan summer. Days are warm and luminously clear — perfect for paragliding and sitting with a chai. But the mountains cool fast once the sun drops, and brief pre-monsoon showers are possible. Layer, and you'll be fine.

☀️
Days25–30°C · Warm & sunny
🌙
Nights15–18°C · A jacket is mandatory
🌦️
ShowersPossible · Short & dramatic

What to Bring

Light breathable clothes for daytime
Warm fleece or jacket — mandatory for evenings
Light rain jacket or compact umbrella
Comfortable walking shoes (uneven terrain)
Sun cream & sunglasses (altitude UV)
One smart outfit for the ceremony
📷 No camera needed — we're providing film cameras!
Small daypack for explorations
What to Wear

Dress Code

There's no strict rule — wear something you feel good in. The ceremony is outdoors on mountain terrain, and the evening gets cool, so comfort matters as much as style. Here's a gentle guide for those who'd like one.

For the Gentlemen
Simple & Sharp

A clean black-and-white combination never fails here — it photographs beautifully against both monastery walls and mountain green. But if you'd like to add a pop of colour or a traditional element, go for it.

Black — always worksWhite — always works

Optional: A coloured pocket square, traditional embroidery, or a kurta if you'd like to lean into the setting.

For the Ladies
Earth & Forest Tones

Tops (or full outfits) in any of the tones below harmonise beautifully with both the Tibetan palette and the mountain landscape. Bottoms can be any colour you love.

Olive
Deep Forest Green
Chocolate
Terracotta

These are suggestions, not rules. Wear what makes you feel yourself.

First Steps in India

Landing in Delhi

If your journey takes you through Indira Gandhi International Airport (DEL), do these two things before you leave the terminal.

📱 Buy a Local SIM Card

Airtel and Jio counters are in the arrivals hall. A tourist SIM with data costs ₹300–500 (about €4–6). Bring your passport. Without a local number, navigating India is considerably harder.

💵 Get Cash in Rupees (INR)

Bir is a village. Most stalls, rickshaws, and small shops are cash-only. Withdraw from an ATM in arrivals or exchange at the counter. ₹3,000–5,000 (€35–60) is plenty for several days.

Normal India thing: ATMs sometimes decline foreign cards on the first try. Just try again, or use another machine. It's the machine, not your card.
The Journey

Getting to Bir

There's no train to Bir — which is part of its charm. You arrive by mountain road. All three routes below are comfortable and well-worn.

Your route to Bir · From Delhi to the Dhauladhar range
✦ Recommended

Option A — Fly to Dharamsala, then Taxi

Fly into Dharamsala / Kangra Airport (DHM) — about 1 hour from Delhi, multiple flights daily. From DHM the drive to Bir takes around 2.5 hours through cedar forests and mountain passes.

Pre-arrange your taxi before landing. We've compiled a list of trusted local drivers — people recommended by the monasteries, known to the family. Find this list in the WhatsApp group. Message your driver 24 hours ahead and confirm the pickup point at DHM arrivals.

🌙 Overnight

Option B — Overnight Volvo Bus from Delhi

Several overnight buses depart Delhi in the early evening, arriving Bir by morning — roughly 12 hours. You sleep, the mountains pass, you arrive.

Arrival point: Sangrai Parking, Bir Colony — we will meet you there. Book via RedBus or HRTC online. Bring a light blanket. [Delhi departure terminal TBC — check WhatsApp group]

🚗 Flexible

Option C — Private Taxi from Delhi

Delhi to Bir in 10–12 hours, your own schedule. The scenery after Mandi is spectacular. Share with another guest and it's quite reasonable. Approximate cost: ₹6,000–9,000. Use the trusted driver list in the group.

Where to Rest

Places to Stay

Book early — Bir is small and June fills up. All options are either at the venue or a short auto-rickshaw ride away.

✦ Wedding Venue

Sleep under Himalayan stars. Genuinely recommended.
Arriving Early

Bir & Beyond — Your Adventure Guide

Some of you are arriving days before — wonderful. Here's your map of the area, and below it, everything worth doing within reach of Bir.

  • 🛕
    Stroll the Tibetan Colony

    Alleys, monasteries, momo stalls, thangka paintings. Sherab Ling Monastery is the spiritual anchor — morning puja begins around 7am. Sit quietly at the back. Tseyang will share etiquette guidance before you arrive.

  • 🌿
    Tea Garden Walk — Morning Light

    Wake early and walk the terraced gardens. The Himalayan morning light here is extraordinary. The tea gardens near Palampur (≈35 km) are particularly beautiful — pair with a visit to the ancient Shiva temple at Baijnath (≈40 min).

  • ☁️
    Watch the Paragliders Land

    Bring chai to the landing field in the colony and watch pilots drift down from Billing against the Dhauladhar. One of those rare sights that stops you mid-sentence.

  • 🏛️
    Dharamshala & McLeod Ganj

    If you want one "big" side trip, this is it — the Dalai Lama's home in exile, prayer wheels, forest kora paths, and cafés that could double as your mountain office. About 3 hours from Bir. We can organise a group trip.

  • 👟
    A Note on Shoes

    For colony walks and temples, comfortable flat shoes are fine. If you're venturing to Billing, Barot Valley, or any trail above — proper hiking or trail shoes are strongly recommended.

🌸
Day 1 · June 20th, 2026
The Ceremony & Fusion Reception

Tibetan blessings at dawn. Ukrainian warmth by evening.

07:30
Shared Transport Departs All Guests

Buses depart from designated guest accommodation points to the monastery. Be at your pickup spot a few minutes early.

08:00 – 11:00
Family Ceremony at the Monastery Tibetan

Traditional Tibetan Buddhist blessings, chanting, and the ceremonial exchange of Khatag scarves — white silk offered as a sign of respect and goodwill. Tseyang will share specific etiquette guidance beforehand — what to wear, how to sit, and when to be still.

11:00 – 11:30
Transfer to Reception Venue Together

Short journey to Sukoon Baag. The farmstay is waiting.

11:30 – 13:30
Greetings & Lunch Reception Together

A joyful daytime gathering at Sukoon Baag with light Indian and Tibetan snacks, cheerful music, and the first proper hellos. No agenda — just the pleasure of being in the same place.

13:30 – 17:00
Free Time & Recovery Rest

Rest, wander, nap in the afternoon warmth. The bride and groom will be taking portraits. The mountains will still be there when you wake up.

17:00 – 19:30
Ukrainian Fusion Celebration Ukrainian

🍞 Коровай (Bread Ceremony): A braided ceremonial bread is presented and shared. In Ukrainian tradition, whoever takes the largest piece leads the household. You've been warned — and invited to try.


🎭 Lighter Moments (×1–3): Ukrainian wedding games that may gently put you on the spot. Participation strongly encouraged.


🕯️ Heartfelt Moments (×1–2): Quiet, meaningful rituals — wishes shared. Have a tissue somewhere accessible.

19:30 – 21:00
Dinner, Music & Fire Together

A long, unhurried dinner. Music. Dancing if the moment calls for it. And if the evening holds — a fire under Himalayan stars. No schedule from here. Stay as long as you like.

🪂
Day 2 · June 21st
The Chill Day

Slow mornings, mountain adventures, your own pace.

09:00 – 11:00
Afterparty Breakfast Close Friends

A slow, informal morning at [Breakfast Venue — TBC]. Chai, stories, and the gentle ritual of reassembling after a celebration. No speeches. Just company.

11:00 Onwards
Choose Your Adventure Your Choice

The rest of the day belongs to you. Three paths:

🪂
Tandem Paragliding

Your included tandem flight from Billing — one of the world's premier paragliding launch points. An instructor goes with you. Meet at the Colony Landing Zone at 11:00.

Included · Billing Takeoff
🏛️
Dharamsala Day Trip

McLeod Ganj — the Dalai Lama's home in exile, 2–3 hours away. Vibrant streets, Tibetan temples, excellent coffee. Group trip can be organised — let us know.

~3 Hours Each Way
⛺
Mountain Camping

For those signed up — overnight departure into the high ground above Bir. Stars, silence, altitude. Details managed separately.

Optional · Pre-signup Required
Your Ground Crew · On the Ground from Start to Finish
Tseyang's Brother is Your Man

From the moment you land in India to the moment you depart — if you need anything at all, Tseyang's brother is your point of contact. Transport, logistics, directions, a reassuring voice when nothing makes sense — he has it handled. Please reach out to him, not to us. We will be a little occupied.

Lost at the bus terminal? Taxi didn't show? Don't know where to go next? He's been expecting your call.

[Brother's Number — TBC]
India Wonders · For the Curious & the Restless
You're Already Here.
Go Further.

India is not a country you visit once and understand. But here's a start — places worth the detour, decisions made easier.

The Classic Circuit

The Golden Triangle

Three cities, wildly different, all within reach of Delhi. This is where most first-time visitors to India go — and they go back. The circuit works because each city gives you something the others don't.

🕌
Delhi
Day 1–2 · Your Gateway

Old Delhi's chaotic lanes, the Red Fort, Humayun's Tomb, the best street food in the country. It's overwhelming at first — then it becomes addictive.

1–2 days · Free–₹500/sight
🏯
Agra
Day 3 · The Taj

The Taj Mahal is one of those rare things that actually exceeds its reputation. Go at sunrise. Skip midday crowds. Agra Fort is excellent and half as visited.

1 day · ₹1,100 entry · 3–4h from Delhi
🎨
Jaipur
Day 4–5 · The Pink City

Amber Fort, the City Palace, block-printed textiles, and the best dal baati you'll eat. Warmer than Delhi, more colourful, slightly slower.

1–2 days · ₹5h from Delhi by train

How Much Time Do You Have?

Your Time
Our Suggestion
2–3 days
Delhi only. Don't rush it. Old Delhi alone is a full day.
4–5 days
Delhi + Agra. Take the Gatimaan Express (90 min, fast & comfortable). Done in a long weekend.
6–7 days
Full Golden Triangle. Delhi → Agra → Jaipur → Delhi. Book trains in advance on IRCTC.
8+ days
Add Varanasi. The most ancient city on earth. Nothing quite prepares you for it.
Book trains early: Indian Railways fills up fast. Use IRCTC.co.in or ask a local agent in Delhi (small fee, saves a lot of confusion).
Why India at All

Absorb, Don't Judge

India requires a particular kind of openness. You don't need to love everything, or understand everything. You just need to stay curious. A civilisation that gave the world yoga, meditation, the decimal system, the concept of zero — and co-invented the USB (Ajay Bhatt, 1994) — deserves a little patience in return.

🧘
Yoga & Meditation

Not a wellness trend — a 3,000-year-old philosophical tradition. The Sanskrit root means "union." Himachal Pradesh is one of its living heartlands.

🔢
The Concept of Zero

Invented here. Without Indian numerals, modern mathematics, computing, and your phone simply wouldn't exist in their current form.

🔌
USB — Co-invented by India

Ajay Bhatt, born in Gujarat, co-invented the Universal Serial Bus in 1994. Every device you've ever plugged in carries a small piece of India.

🙏
The Head Wobble

A sideways tilt meaning yes, okay, understood, or I'm listening. Do not mistake it for disagreement. It is a sign of warm engagement.

Eat Well, Eat Safe

A Short Guide to Indian Food

Indian food is one of the great cuisines of the world. Start conservative, graduate to adventurous.

✓ Start with These

🥛
Lassi

Chilled yoghurt drink — sweet (mango, rose) or salted. The mango lassi is basically dessert.

Must Try
🫓
Roti / Chapati

Unleavened flatbread, fresh off the tawa. Tear and scoop. The base of everything.

Staple
🍚
Chicken Biryani

Slow-cooked spiced basmati with chicken — aromatic, complex, deeply satisfying.

Classic
🍗
Afghan Chicken

Yoghurt-marinated, mildly spiced — excellent for those calibrating their spice tolerance.

Beginner-Friendly
🫘
Dal Tadka

Yellow lentils with garlic and ghee. Vegetarian comfort food. Reliable everywhere.

Vegetarian Star
🥘
Paneer Butter Masala

Fresh cheese in a rich, mildly spiced tomato-cream sauce. The most universally loved vegetarian dish.

Crowd Pleaser

⚠ Navigate with Care

🚰
Tap Water

Never directly. Always buy sealed mineral water. Ice at restaurants is usually fine; street ice — skip it.

Avoid Directly
🥗
Raw Salads / Cut Fruit (Stalls)

Likely washed in tap water. Stick to cooked food for the first few days while your gut adjusts.

First Few Days
🌶️
Spice Escalation

"Medium" in India is not what you expect. Always ask for mild first, then adjust.

Pace Yourself
🍖
Pork & Beef

Rarely available in Buddhist and Hindu areas like Bir. Chicken, lamb, and vegetarian dishes are excellent here.

Limited in Bir
The golden rule: Eat where locals eat in large numbers. A crowded dhaba with plastic chairs and fast turnover is almost always safer — and better — than an empty tourist restaurant with a laminated menu.
༄
With love, from the mountains.

Yaroslav & Tseyang · Bir, Himachal Pradesh · June 2026
